items.prompt_md: In an O(1) insert/delete/getRandom design, why is a hash map paired with a dynamic array instead of using either one alone?
options: The hash map gives O(1) lookup of a value's position, while the array gives O(1) indexed access needed to pick a uniformly random element
The array sorts itself automatically so getRandom can binary search for a random position
The hash map stores every value in random order already, so getRandom just returns its first entry
The array alone can already delete any value in O(1) without help from a hash map
items.answer: The hash map gives O(1) lookup of a value's position, while the array gives O(1) indexed access needed to pick a uniformly random element
items.explanation_md: getRandom needs indexable storage (the array), and O(1) delete/insert by value needs a way to find that value's slot without scanning (the hash map) — neither structure alone offers both.

items.prompt_md: What invariant must the hash map and the array agree on at every point in this design?
options: The hash map's entry for a value must always equal that value's current index in the array
The array must always be sorted by value
The hash map must contain every index the array has ever held, including removed ones
The array's length must always equal the hash map's capacity, not its size
items.answer: The hash map's entry for a value must always equal that value's current index in the array
items.explanation_md: If the map's stored index for a value ever drifts from where that value actually sits in the array, both delete and access break — that index correspondence is the entire invariant.

items.prompt_md: How does deleting an arbitrary value from the array stay O(1) instead of O(n)?
options: Swap the target value with the array's last element, update the map for the moved element's new index, then pop the last slot
Shift every element after the target one position to the left
Rebuild the array from scratch, skipping the deleted value
Mark the slot as deleted but never actually shrink the array
items.answer: Swap the target value with the array's last element, update the map for the moved element's new index, then pop the last slot
items.explanation_md: Swap-with-last turns removal into 'overwrite one slot and shrink by one' — no shifting — as long as the moved element's new index is written back into the map.

items.prompt_md: A buggy remove() swaps the target value to the end and pops it, but skips one step. What bug results?
options: It never updates the hash map's stored index for the element that got moved into the removed slot, so that element's map entry now points at the wrong (or dead) index
It crashes immediately on the next insert
It causes getRandom to always return the same element
It causes insert to run in O(n) forever after
items.answer: It never updates the hash map's stored index for the element that got moved into the removed slot, so that element's map entry now points at the wrong (or dead) index
items.explanation_md: Skipping the map update for the moved element leaves it 'pointing' at an index that no longer holds it, so a later removal or lookup for that value silently touches the wrong slot.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of insert, remove, and getRandom in the hash-map + dynamic-array design (amortized)?
options: O(1) for all three
O(log n) for all three
O(n) for remove, O(1) for the rest
O(1) for insert and getRandom, O(n) for remove
items.answer: O(1) for all three
items.explanation_md: Hash map lookup/insert, array append, swap-with-last, and indexed random access are each O(1) amortized, so every operation lands at O(1).

items.prompt_md: Why can't a plain hash set alone support getRandom in O(1)?
options: A hash set has no notion of position or index, so picking a uniformly random element requires either scanning all entries or first copying them into an indexable structure
Hash sets store elements in sorted order already, so a random pick would be biased
Hash sets cannot hold more than one element of a given type
Hash sets already support O(1) getRandom natively
items.answer: A hash set has no notion of position or index, so picking a uniformly random element requires either scanning all entries or first copying them into an indexable structure
items.explanation_md: Without indexable slots, 'pick element number k' has no O(1) meaning in a hash set — that's exactly the gap the paired dynamic array fills.

items.prompt_md: Why can't a plain dynamic array alone support O(1) delete-by-value?
options: It first has to scan for the value's position (O(n)) since arrays don't offer O(1) lookup by value, only by index
Arrays can only be appended to, never shrunk
Arrays require re-sorting after every deletion
Arrays already support O(1) delete-by-value on their own
items.answer: It first has to scan for the value's position (O(n)) since arrays don't offer O(1) lookup by value, only by index
items.explanation_md: An array gives O(1) access if you already know the index — finding that index for an arbitrary value is the part a hash map is needed for.

items.prompt_md: Why maintain two heaps (a max-heap for the lower half, a min-heap for the upper half) instead of one fully sorted list for a running median?
options: Inserting into a fully sorted list costs O(n) to find the insertion point and shift elements, while pushing into a heap costs only O(log n)
Two heaps use less total memory than one sorted list
A sorted list cannot represent a median at all
Heaps automatically stay balanced with no extra bookkeeping
items.answer: Inserting into a fully sorted list costs O(n) to find the insertion point and shift elements, while pushing into a heap costs only O(log n)
items.explanation_md: The whole motivation is speed: a sorted list's O(n) insert is exactly what the two-heap split avoids, trading it for O(log n) heap pushes.

items.prompt_md: What invariant must hold between the two heaps at every point?
options: Every value in the max-heap (low half) is <= every value in the min-heap (high half), and the two heaps' sizes differ by at most one
The max-heap must always be strictly larger than the min-heap
Both heaps must contain exactly the same values, duplicated
The min-heap must contain only even numbers and the max-heap only odd numbers
items.answer: Every value in the max-heap (low half) is <= every value in the min-heap (high half), and the two heaps' sizes differ by at most one
items.explanation_md: Those two facts together are what let the median be read straight off the heap tops — break either one and the tops stop being the true middle values.

items.prompt_md: After pushing a new number into one of the heaps, when must you move an element across to the other heap?
options: Whenever the sizes drift apart by more than one, pop the root of the larger heap and push it into the smaller one to restore balance
Only when the new number is exactly equal to the current median
Never — once assigned to a heap, a number stays there permanently
Whenever the smaller heap is completely empty, regardless of the size gap
items.answer: Whenever the sizes drift apart by more than one, pop the root of the larger heap and push it into the smaller one to restore balance
items.explanation_md: Rebalancing on a size gap greater than one is what keeps the two-heap sizes within one of each other, which is required for O(1) median reads.

items.prompt_md: For a running median maintained with two heaps, what are the time complexities of addNum and findMedian?
options: addNum is O(log n), findMedian is O(1)
addNum is O(1), findMedian is O(log n)
Both addNum and findMedian are O(n)
Both addNum and findMedian are O(log n)
items.answer: addNum is O(log n), findMedian is O(1)
items.explanation_md: Pushing/popping a heap costs O(log n); once balanced, the median is just one or two heap-top peeks, which is O(1).

items.prompt_md: A buggy median-finder pushes each new number directly into whichever heap 'seems right' by size alone, skipping the usual push-then-cross-move step. What breaks?
options: A number can land in the wrong half (e.g. a large value pushed straight into the low/max-heap), violating the max(low) <= min(high) ordering and corrupting the median
The heaps immediately throw an out-of-memory error
addNum becomes O(n) but the median stays correct
findMedian starts requiring O(n) time instead of O(1)
items.answer: A number can land in the wrong half (e.g. a large value pushed straight into the low/max-heap), violating the max(low) <= min(high) ordering and corrupting the median
items.explanation_md: Choosing a heap by size alone, without first pushing to one heap and popping its extreme across, can plant a value on the wrong side of the split and silently break the ordering invariant.

items.prompt_md: Why does add-and-search-word-with-wildcards call for a trie rather than a hash set of the inserted words?
options: A wildcard character must match any letter at that position, which means branching over every possible child at that trie node — a hash set of whole words offers no way to explore 'any character here' without checking every stored word
A trie uses less memory than a hash set no matter how many words are stored
A hash set cannot store more than 26 words
Hash sets don't support insertion, only lookup
items.answer: A wildcard character must match any letter at that position, which means branching over every possible child at that trie node — a hash set of whole words offers no way to explore 'any character here' without checking every stored word
items.explanation_md: The trie's branching structure is exactly what makes 'try every child at this position' cheap when a wildcard is hit — a flat hash set of full words has no per-character structure to explore.

items.prompt_md: For a trie holding words up to length L, what is the time complexity of search() when the query contains k wildcard characters, in the worst case?
options: O(L)
O(log L)
O(1) regardless of k
items.explanation_md: Each wildcard can branch into every one of the alphabet's children, so in the worst case the search explores an exponential number of paths in k, each up to length L.

items.prompt_md: A trie-based word search returns true for 'car' even though only 'card' and 'care' were ever inserted (never 'car' itself). What's the likely bug?
options: The search doesn't check the end-of-word marker on the final node — it treats simply reaching a node with children as a match, instead of requiring that node be flagged as a completed word
The trie was built with the wrong alphabet size
The wildcard character was inserted as a literal letter
The hash map backing the trie ran out of capacity
items.answer: The search doesn't check the end-of-word marker on the final node — it treats simply reaching a node with children as a match, instead of requiring that node be flagged as a completed word
items.explanation_md: A trie node existing on the path only means some longer word passes through it — search must also confirm that node was explicitly marked as a word's end, or prefixes get falsely accepted.

items.prompt_md: A collection must support inserting a value, deleting an arbitrary value, and returning a uniformly random element currently in the collection — all in average O(1) time.
options: Hash map + dynamic array (swap-with-last)
Two heaps (max-heap + min-heap)
Hash map + doubly linked list
Trie
items.answer: Hash map + dynamic array (swap-with-last)
items.explanation_md: O(1) getRandom needs indexable storage (the array) and O(1) delete-by-value needs a locator (the hash map) — that pairing, with swap-with-last on delete, is the signature for this exact trio of operations.

items.prompt_md: Numbers arrive one at a time in a stream, and after every arrival you must be able to report the median of everything seen so far, without rescanning the whole stream.
options: Two heaps (max-heap + min-heap)
Hash map + dynamic array (swap-with-last)
Single hash map
Trie
items.answer: Two heaps (max-heap + min-heap)
items.explanation_md: A running median over an ever-growing stream, without full rescans, is the two-heap trigger: max-heap for the lower half, min-heap for the upper half, rebalanced after each insert.

items.prompt_md: You must support inserting words and later searching for a word where any position may instead be a wildcard that matches any single letter.
options: Trie
Two heaps (max-heap + min-heap)
Hash map + dynamic array (swap-with-last)
Single hash map
items.answer: Trie
items.explanation_md: A wildcard that can match any character at a position requires exploring every branch at that node — a trie's per-character structure is what makes that exploration tractable.

items.prompt_md: You need O(1) insert and O(1) 'have I seen this value before' checks — no deletions will ever happen, and you never need to fetch a random element.
options: Single hash map
Hash map + dynamic array (swap-with-last)
Two heaps (max-heap + min-heap)
Trie
items.answer: Single hash map
items.explanation_md: Trap: without a deletion or random-pick requirement, the paired dynamic array earns nothing — a plain hash map/set already answers membership in O(1) by itself.

items.prompt_md: You need to record whole words and later check, in O(1), whether an exact word (never a wildcard, never a prefix) was previously recorded.
options: Single hash map
Trie
Two heaps (max-heap + min-heap)
Hash map + doubly linked list
items.answer: Single hash map
items.explanation_md: Trap: a trie only pays for itself once you need per-character exploration (wildcards or prefixes) — for exact whole-word membership alone, a plain hash set is simpler and just as fast.

items.prompt_md: You need O(1) insert and O(1) delete-by-value from a collection, but there is no requirement to ever fetch a random element or preserve any particular order.
options: Single hash map
Hash map + dynamic array (swap-with-last)
Trie
Two heaps (max-heap + min-heap)
items.answer: Single hash map
items.explanation_md: Trap: the swap-with-last array only exists to make getRandom possible — drop that requirement and a plain hash set already gives O(1) insert and O(1) delete on its own.

items.prompt_md: A shuffled-deck simulator must let you add a card back into the deck, remove a specific card, and draw a uniformly random remaining card, each in O(1).
options: Hash map + dynamic array (swap-with-last)
Trie
Two heaps (max-heap + min-heap)
Hash map + doubly linked list
items.answer: Hash map + dynamic array (swap-with-last)
items.explanation_md: Insert, delete-by-value, and uniform random draw together are exactly the three operations the hash-map-plus-array pairing targets, with the array enabling O(1) random access.

items.prompt_md: A monitoring dashboard ingests a continuous stream of latency readings and must expose the current median on demand, updated after every new reading, without ever sorting the full history.
options: Two heaps (max-heap + min-heap)
Single hash map
Trie
Hash map + dynamic array (swap-with-last)
items.answer: Two heaps (max-heap + min-heap)
items.explanation_md: 'Current median, updated per reading, no full re-sort' is the two-heap fingerprint — each insert costs O(log n) and the median stays a O(1) peek at the two heap tops.

items.prompt_md: A spell-checker must support adding dictionary words and then answering queries where the user is unsure of one letter and marks it with a placeholder that should match anything.
options: Trie
Hash map + dynamic array (swap-with-last)
Two heaps (max-heap + min-heap)
Single hash map
items.answer: Trie
items.explanation_md: An unsure letter that must match anything is a wildcard search, which needs the trie's ability to branch into every child at that position — a flat word set can't do that without scanning everything.

items.prompt_md: A cache must support O(1) access to a key's value and, once full, must evict whichever key has gone the longest without being touched — there's no need for random access or median queries here.
options: Hash map + doubly linked list
Hash map + dynamic array (swap-with-last)
Two heaps (max-heap + min-heap)
Trie
items.answer: Hash map + doubly linked list
items.explanation_md: Recency-based eviction with O(1) access is the LRU signature (hash map + doubly linked list) — don't reach for the random-pick or median-tracking pairings when the actual requirement is 'oldest untouched key goes first.'
